title: Noise: A Flaw in Human Judgment
author: Daniel Kahneman, Olivier Sibony, Cass R. Sunstein
excerpt: Wherever humans make judgments—from courtrooms to boardrooms, medical clinics to hiring committees—unwanted variability called 'noise' produces errors just as damaging as bias, yet it remains largely invisible. Kahneman, Sibony, and Sunstein reveal how to recognize noise and reduce it through decision hygiene.
keyIdeas: Noise (unwanted variability in judgment) is as harmful as bias
Organizations are systematically blind to the noise in their own decisions
Noise can be reduced through 'decision hygiene' without eliminating valuable diversity
Where there is judgment, there is noise—and it is usually more than we expect
keyTakeaways: Audit your decisions for noise before trying to reduce bias
Replace unstructured judgment with structured guidelines where possible
Use the 'noise audit' to measure inconsistency in organizational decisions
Aggregate multiple independent judgments to reduce noise
whoShouldRead: Leaders and managers who make or oversee important decisions
Professionals in medicine, law, finance, and HR
Anyone who enjoyed Thinking, Fast and Slow
Organizational leaders concerned about fairness and consistency
whoShouldSkip: Readers who think only bias matters in judgment
Those looking for individual decision-making tips (focuses on organizational context)
